primary_diagnosis.name: Cerumen impaction, right ear (acute-on-chronic, water-exacerbated hygroscopic expansion) causing acute conductive hearing loss

pathophysiology_summary: Dry impacted cerumen in the right external auditory canal absorbed water during the shower and underwent hygroscopic (sponge-like) expansion, completely occluding the canal. Complete obstruction produces acute conductive hearing loss, a pressure/blocked sensation with throbbing otalgia, and tinnitus; vagal (Arnold's) stimulation of the canal wall by the impacted plug produces a reflex dry cough. Recent cotton-bud use packed the wax deeper and abraded the canal (reactive erythema). The central telehealth problem is that a SUDDEN unilateral hearing loss is indistinguishable, on history alone, from sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL — a time-critical emergency where corticosteroid treatment is most effective within ~72 hours) and from tympanic-membrane perforation. Only direct otoscopy (a complete wax plug, eardrum not visualised) and tuning-fork testing (Weber lateralising to the affected ear = conductive loss) separate these — and neither can be performed over chat.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden complete blockage right after water exposure, on a background of weeks of ear fullness and itch, with a plugged/pressure feeling — classic for hygroscopic expansion of pre-existing cerumen; but it cannot be confirmed without otoscopy.

Any sudden unilateral hearing loss must trigger SSNHL consideration. It presents identically on history and is time-critical (steroid window). It cannot be excluded without tuning-fork testing and/or audiometry.
Water exposure and a sudden change in hearing raise the possibility of perforation; the eardrum cannot be seen without otoscopy so it cannot be excluded remotely.
Water exposure, canal itch and a blocked painful ear are compatible with otitis externa; distinguished on examination.
Otalgia with hearing change; less likely without a preceding upper-respiratory illness or fever, but not excludable on history alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At telehealth intake the AI Doctor cannot yet separate benign conductive causes from SSNHL — the correct posture is to arrange examination, not to anchor on 'ear wax'.

differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag screen negative (no true vertigo, no facial weakness, no ear bleeding/discharge, afebrile, no mastoid pain); the benign conductive picture is now most likely — but confirmation still requires otoscopy and tuning-fork testing.
Still cannot be excluded on history and remains the do-not-miss diagnosis until the ear is examined and a Weber/Rinne test is performed.
Afebrile with no post-auricular pain or swelling and no discharge argues strongly against suppurative middle-ear/mastoid infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative red-flag screen makes benign cerumen most likely but does NOT license a remote diagnosis — SSNHL is excluded only by examination.

differential.evidence_basis: In-person otoscopy shows the right canal completely occluded by a large, hard, dark-brown cerumen plug with the eardrum not visualised; Weber lateralises to the affected (right) ear, confirming a conductive loss and excluding a sensorineural cause.
Weber lateralising to the affected ear indicates conductive, not sensorineural, loss — SSNHL is excluded once the ear is examined.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The diagnosis becomes secure only AFTER in-person examination — which is exactly why the telehealth-correct action is to arrange that examination promptly.

red_flags.description: Sudden unilateral hearing loss — indistinguishable on history from sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), which cannot be examined over chat.
red_flags.clinical_significance: SSNHL is a time-critical otological emergency (corticosteroids most effective within ~72 hours). Because it presents identically to benign conductive loss on history, a sudden unilateral loss mandates prompt in-person examination rather than remote reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'ear wax' and treating remotely could delay steroid treatment for an unrecognised SSNHL, risking permanent hearing loss.

red_flags.description: True room-spinning vertigo (as opposed to mild unsteadiness).
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe vertigo would suggest inner-ear or central vestibular pathology rather than simple canal obstruction and would raise the escalation tier.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing genuine vertigo could delay recognition of labyrinthine or central disease.

red_flags.description: Facial droop/weakness or bleeding/discharge from the ear.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Facial-nerve involvement or otorrhoea/bleeding points to a more serious process (e.g. cholesteatoma, herpes zoster oticus, trauma, malignant otitis externa) requiring urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking facial weakness could delay treatment of a serious otological or neurological cause.

red_flags.description: Fever with severe pain or swelling over the mastoid (behind the ear), or foul-smelling discharge.
red_flags.clinical_significance: These indicate acute mastoiditis or a complicated middle-ear infection requiring urgent in-person/ED care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated mastoiditis can progress to intracranial complications.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'just ear wax' from the history and giving self-care without arranging examination — a sudden unilateral hearing loss can be sensorineural (SSNHL), which is time-critical and cannot be excluded remotely.

Advising ear syringing or irrigation on a hard, completely obstructing plug, or while the eardrum cannot be seen and a perforation has not been excluded — risks pain, canal trauma and, if the drum is perforated, middle-ear injury.
Recommending cotton buds or manual removal pushes wax deeper against the eardrum and can perforate it.
Escalating uncomplicated cerumen to an ambulance/ED when no red flag (true vertigo, facial weakness, bleeding, fever with mastoid pain) is present is an over-triage error.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Otoscopic view of the ear canal and tympanic membrane
Whether the tympanic membrane is intact or perforated
Weber/Rinne tuning-fork lateralisation (conductive vs sensorineural loss)
The nature and hardness of any obstructing material
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange a same-day in-person ear examination (GP or after-hours clinic with an otoscope) to visualise the canal and eardrum and perform tuning-fork testing, so as to exclude sudden sensorineural hearing loss and perforation; escalate to ED/000 if true vertigo, facial weakness, bleeding/discharge from the ear, or fever with pain behind the ear develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High — the dominant risk is anchoring on benign cerumen and missing sudden sensorineural hearing loss, which presents identically on history and is time-critical.
